perturbation.original_text: Prior to the disclosure of any Conﬁdential Information to any such Personnel, the Receiving Party shall inform them of the conﬁdential nature of the material and of the provisions of this conﬁdentiality undertaking and obtain written conﬁrmation of their acceptance of these terms.
perturbation.changed_text: Prior to the disclosure of any Conﬁdential Information to any such Personnel, the Receiving Party is encouraged to inform them of the conﬁdential nature of the material.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall' to 'is encouraged to,' we remove the mandatory requirement for the Receiving Party to inform its Personnel about the confidentiality agreement and obtain written confirmation. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 5.1, which states that the Receiving Party 'shall procure that its Personnel do not' disclose Confidential Information, as there's now no enforceable mechanism to ensure Personnel are aware of and bound by the confidentiality undertaking. The term 'shall procure' implies a necessary action, this directly contradicts the change.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Save as otherwise permitted herein, a Receiving Party shall not, and shall procure that its Personnel do not, at any time without the Disclosing Party’s prior written consent: (a) disclose the Conﬁdential Information to any third party, either directly or indirectly; or (b) disclose to any person: - (i) the fact that discussions or negotiations are taking place between the Parties; (ii) the content of any such discussions or negotiations; (iii) any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to the Purpose; or (iv) that Conﬁdential Information has been requested or received, unless required to do so by law or by the order or ruling of a Court or tribunal or regulatory body or recognised stock exchange of competent jurisdiction, in which case, if the Receiving Party is required to disclose such Conﬁdential Information it shall, unless prohibited from doing so, notify the Disclosing Party promptly in writing of that fact and, in any event, prior to making such disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated the Receiving Party to inform its personnel about the confidential information and obtain written confirmation. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'is encouraged to,' thus removing the mandatory requirement. This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which states the Receiving Party 'shall procure that its Personnel do not' disclose confidential information. The weakened obligation makes it difficult to enforce the 'shall procure' clause, because there is no longer a guaranteed mechanism to ensure Personnel are aware of, or bound by, the confidentiality undertaking.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall, immediately upon receipt of Notice to that eﬀect: (a) return to the Disclosing Party all Conﬁdential Information (including all copies held); (b) destroy those portions (and all copies) of any analyses, compilations, studies, reports or other documents prepared by it for its use containing or reﬂecting or generated from, in whole or in part, any Conﬁdential Information; and (c) expunge and destroy any Conﬁdential Information from any computer, word processor or other device in its possession or custody or control containing such information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may, upon request, return some Conﬁdential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall' to 'may' and replacing the detailed destruction requirements with a vague statement about returning 'some' Confidential Information, we contradict the core principle of protecting disclosed information after the agreement terminates. This contradicts Section 3.1, which mandates that the Receiving Party 'treat and safeguard as private and conﬁdential all of the Conﬁdential Information,' as there's now no guarantee all information will be returned or destroyed, increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure. This makes the original safeguarding commitment in section 3.1 unenforceable. As 'shall' dictates the procedure and 'may' makes the action permissable, they stand in contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: A Receiving Party shall treat and safeguard as private and conﬁdential all of the Conﬁdential Information and will take all reasonable precautions in dealing with any Conﬁdential Information so as to prevent any third party from having access to the Conﬁdential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the Receiving Party to return or destroy all Confidential Information upon notice. The changed text alters this obligation by stating the Receiving Party 'may' return 'some' Confidential Information. This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which requires the Receiving Party to 'treat and safeguard as private and conﬁdential all of the Conﬁdential Information,' creating a strong inconsistency because it undermines the obligation to protect all confidential information by allowing the receiving party to only return some information. This is an Omissions - In Text Contradiction as essential language is removed, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections. Since the original 'shall' statement has been changed to 'may', the language now permits a violation of the original safeguard, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party hereby acknowledges and agrees that damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this undertaking and a Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the remedies of injunction, speciﬁc performance and other equitable relief for any threatened or actual breach of any such undertaking.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that damages would be an adequate remedy for any breach of this undertaking.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that 'damages would be an adequate remedy,' we directly contradict the original clause that explicitly denies damages as sufficient and grants the Disclosing Party the right to equitable relief (injunction, specific performance). This change eliminates the Disclosing Party's ability to seek immediate court intervention to prevent further disclosure of confidential information, weakening their protection. The change from the 'not adequate remedy' to 'adequate remedy' creates an intext contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party hereby acknowledges and agrees that damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach of this undertaking and a Disclosing Party shall be entitled to the remedies of injunction, speciﬁc performance and other equitable relief for any threatened or actual breach of any such undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The changed text states that 'damages would be an adequate remedy', which directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that 'damages would not be an adequate remedy'. This creates a direct conflict within the document regarding the appropriate remedy for a breach, making enforcement unclear and indicating a strong in-text contradiction, as per the provided explanation and the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties irrevocably agree that the English courts shall have the exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement. Each Party agrees to waive any objection to the English courts, whether on the grounds of venue, or on the grounds that the forum is not appropriate.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement will be subject to mediation before any legal action.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause specifying English courts as having exclusive jurisdiction and replacing it with a mediation requirement, we create an in-text contradiction. While mediation isn't necessarily incompatible with legal jurisdiction, removing the exclusive jurisdiction clause leaves the legal venue undefined. This contradicts the implied agreement of legal proceedings in English courts, creating ambiguity about where disputes will ultimately be resolved if mediation fails. The change from 'English courts' to 'mediation' is a direct contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Parties irrevocably agree that the English courts shall have the exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute arising out of or in connection with this Agreement. Each Party agrees to waive any objection to the English courts, whether on the grounds of venue, or on the grounds that the forum is not appropriate.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies the English courts as having exclusive jurisdiction. The changed text removes this exclusivity, replacing it with a requirement for mediation. This directly contradicts the original agreement on exclusive jurisdiction of English courts, creating uncertainty about the legal venue if mediation fails. The contradicted text is the original text which specifies 'English courts' and by removing it there is a contradiction as the Parties have already agreed on a specific venue.
